questions.question: What is the reality gap, and why train in simulation anyway?
options: The lag between sim frames and real time

The systematic difference between sim and real distributions — bearings have more friction than MuJoCo models, motors have unmodeled delays and backlash; but sim offers unlimited resets, parallelism, and no broken hardware, so deployed robot RL must cross the gap
The cost difference between sim and robots
A rendering artifact in Isaac Sim
questions.explanation: A policy that fails on hardware memorized the simulator. A real biped that falls once breaks hardware — but learning to walk takes millions of episodes, so sim is non-negotiable.

questions.question: How does domain randomization work, and what's its failure mode?
options: It adds noise to the test set

Randomize every sim parameter that might differ on hardware (masses, friction, motor gains, sensor noise, lighting) so the policy generalizes across the span — but over-randomizing produces a universal yet overly cautious policy; too much noise acts like too much regularization
It randomizes the network weights
It shuffles training episodes
questions.explanation: Tobin 2017, Peng 2018. If the real robot falls inside the training envelope, the policy works — with zero real data. One recipe, many robots.

questions.question: How do system identification and domain adaptation complement DR?
options: They are obsolete alternatives

SI fits sim parameters to measured real data before training (precise, but un-identified effects like motor deadband still break deployment); adaptation fine-tunes with small real datasets — residual simulators (Real2Sim2Real) or learned observation mappings from real to sim-like inputs
Both require retraining from scratch
They only apply to vision tasks
questions.explanation: The 2026 dominant recipe combines all three with massively parallel simulation rather than picking one.

questions.question: What is privileged teacher-student learning?
options: Larger models teaching smaller ones text

Train a teacher in sim with ground-truth privileged information (friction, terrain height), then distill a student that sees only real-sensor observations — the student learns to INFER privileged features from observation history (Miki 2022, ANYmal)
Human teleoperation as supervision
Curriculum learning over level difficulty
questions.explanation: The teacher solves an easier fully-observed problem; the student inherits the behavior while remaining deployable on what the hardware can actually sense.

questions.question: What changed with massively parallel simulation (Isaac Lab, MJX, Brax)?
options: Simulation became unnecessary

Thousands of robots run in parallel on one GPU — PPO with 4,096 humanoids collects years of experience in hours, and domain randomization becomes almost free when each env carries different randomized parameters
Training moved entirely to CPUs
The reality gap fully closed
questions.explanation: The full 2026 quadruped recipe: parallel DR sim → privileged teacher → proprioception-only student → optional observation adaptation → zero-shot deploy, with minutes of safety-constrained fine-tuning as fallback.
